content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
February 9,
1994
Honorable Patricio Miranda
City of Irwindale.
5050 North Irwindale Avenue
Irwindale,
CA
91706
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—94—014
Dear Councilmember Miranda:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
concerning your duties under the conflict—of—interest provisions
of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”)
1 as
a member of the
Irwindale Redevelopment Agency Board of Directors.
QUESTION
May you participate in decisions of the Irwindale Community
Redevelopment Agency Board of Directors to develop guidelines for
construction of
a
Low— and Moderate—Income housing project and for
selection of the developer for the project?
CONCLUSION
You may participate in the decisions so long as the decisions
will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on your real property or your sources of
income.
FACTS
Several years ago,
the Irwindale Community Redevelopment
Agency
(the “Agency”)
purchased property for the development of
thirty—six housing units for sale to
Low— and Moderate—Income
residents.
The Agency has cleared the property and is
constructing public improvements.
The Agency will shortly be
reviewing development guidelines and selecting a developer for the
project.
You are a councilmember of the City of Irwindale and a member
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A—94—014
Page
2
of the Agency Board of Directors.
The City of Irwindale has
approximately 1,050 residents and less than
300 residential units.
You
own commercial property in the City of Irwindale which is
approximately 465 feet from the Agency-owned development site.
According to the map you have provided with your letter, it
appears this property is located at 5014 North Irwindale Avenue.
The commercial property is leased and operated as
a tire
business.
2
ANALYSIS
Economic Interests
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
As
a member of the
Irwindale City Council
and as
a member of the Agency Board of
Directors,
you are
a public official under the Act.
(Section
82048.)
Section 87103 specifies that
a public official has
a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have
a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:
(b)
Any real property in which the
public official has
a direct or indirect
interest worth one thousand dollars
($1000)
or
more.
Cc)
Any source of
income,
other than
gifts and other than loans
by
a commercial
lending institution in the regular course of
business
on terms available to the public
without regard to official status,
aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in
value provided to,
received by or promised to
the public official within
12 months prior to
2
The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing
advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71.)
However,
Assistant City Attorney William P.
Curley,
III,
previously
provided information concerning this property in connection with a
prior request for advice recently submitted on your behalf.
Curley Advice Letter,
No.
A-93-482.)
To the extent that these are
not the underlying facts,
you should seek further assistance.
In
the future, please include specific information with respect to
the property in question even if you have previously requested
advice concerning the same property.


Our File No.
A-94-014
Page3
V
the time when the decision is made.
*
*
*
*
Section 87103(b)
and
(c).
In your letter,
you state that you own commercial property
less than 2,500 feet from the project site.
Presumably,
you have
an interest in the property in an amount which exceeds $1,000.
(Section 87103(b).)
In addition,
for purposes of Section
87103 (c),
your business tenant is
a source of income to you if the
tenant has made
a payment of
$250 or more to you in the past
12
months.
Therefore,
you may not participate in Agency decisions
3
if the decisions would have
a reasonably foreseeable
4 material
financial effect on your property or your sources of income.
Governmental Decisions
Generally,
each governmental decision must be analyzed
independently to determine if there will be
a foreseeable material
financial effect on an official’s economic interests.
(In re
Owen
(1976)
2
FPPC Ops.
77.)
Thus,
even if you may have
a conflict of
interest with respect to the development as
a whole,
some
decisions may be merely implementation decisions of prior major
policy decisions which may be separable from the decisions
concerning the project as
a whole.
(Martello Advice Letter,
No.
A—92—471.)
V
For example,
in the Athan Advice Letter,
No.
A-86-094,
we
advised:
[W]e conclude that Mayor Bennett must
disqualify himself from participating in decisions
of the San Rarnon City Council/Redevelopment Agency
concerning the proposed Crow Canyon area.
However,
we emphasize that this conclusion applies only to
the malor policy decisions about the project,
such
as project boundaries,
financing decisions,
approval of the environmental impact report,
types
V
of uses,
and malor public improvements in the
project area.
Once the basic policy decisions have
been reached,
Mayor Bennett may participate in the
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed to
‘evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
To the
extent that you may have participated in such decisions,
our
advice is prospective in nature.
An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is
a
substantial likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not
required.
However,
if
an effect is only a mere possibility, it is
not reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
198.)


Our File No. A-94-014
Page4
decisions which implement,
but do not change these
policies.
For example, if the City Council/Redevelopment
Agency votes to conduct an environmental impact
report
(a basic decision from which Mayor Bennett
must disqualify himself),
Mayor Bennett may
participate in the decision to choose the engineer
or consultant to whom the City will award the
contract to perform the EIR.
Emphasis added.
Thus, if the decisions before the Agency include major policy
decisions such as a financing plan for construction,
and such
decisions could result in the termination or a significant
modification of the housing project as a whole,
you must refrain
from participating in it to the extent that the total effect of
the project will materially affect one your interests.
Advice Letter,
No.
A-89-454.)
However, if the decisions are ones
which merely implement major policy decisions previously made,
the
effect of the decisions could be measured by assessing the
difference between various implementation alternatives presented.
(Vickers Advice Letter,
No.
A-84-302;
Mang Advice Letter,
No.
A—85—082.)
You state in your letter that the Agency purchased the
property several years ago for development of
36 hous-ing units.
Thus, it would appear that the decisions before the Agency
pertaining to the development of construction guidelines and the
selection of a developer for construction of the project are
implementation decisions which will not affect the existence of
the project as a whole.
Thus,
if the implementation decisions
will not independently materially affect your economic interests,
you may participate in the decisions.
Materiality
The standard for materiality differs depending on the type of
economic interest involved and whether the economic interest is
directly or indirectly affected by the decision.
Regulation
18702.1 provides definitions for when economic interests are
directly involved in a decision.
(Seg,
Regulation 18702.1(a)
and
(b).)
According to your facts, it appears your interests are only
indirectly affected.
You have provided no facts which indicate that an applicant for
the construction of the project is an economic interest of yours.
If an applicant is an economic interest of yours,
the decisions
concerning the selection of a developer for the project would be
deemed to have direct material financial effect on your economic
interests pursuant to Regulation 18702.1 and your disqualification
would be required.


Our File No.
A—94-014
Page
5
A.
Real Property Interest
You
own commercial property in the jurisdiction which is
within 2,500 feet of the housing project.
The relevant standard
to determine whether there is
a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on your real property interest is Regulation
18702.3.
Pursuant to the standards set forth in the regulation,
you may not participate in decisions which will foreseeably
increase or decrease the fair market value of your real property
by $10,000 or more or the rental value of your property by at
least $1,000 in a
12—month period.
(Regulation 18702.3(a) (3) (A).)
If none of these effects would occur with respect to the real
property,
your property would not be a disqualifying financial
interest under the Act.
B.
Business Tenant
In addition to the above,
your commercial property is leased
and operated as
a business.
Therefore,
your tenant is
a source of
income to you pursuant to Section 87103(c).
Regulation
18702.2
provides the standards for determining materiality where
a source
of
income which is
a business entity is indirectly affected by
a
governmental decision.
Thus,
your disqualification may be
required,
for example,
if your business tenant would be indirectly
materially affected by the Agency’s decisions.
Such determination depends
on the financial size of the
business entity which is the tenant.
For example,
Regulation
18702.2(g)
provides that for
a relatively small business entity,
the indirect effect of
a decision is material where:
(1)
The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for
a fiscal year
of $10,000
or more;
or
(2)
The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for
a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more;
or
(3)
The decision will result in the increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.
We cannot make the factual determination as to whether the
Agency decisions will materially affect a business tenant of
yours.
We must leave this factual determination of materiality to
you within the guidelines provided by Regulation 18702.2.
However,
if subdivision
(g)
of the regulation would apply and the
decisions will not affect the gross revenues of the business
by
$10,000 or more for
a fiscal year,
or the value of its assets or
liabilities by $10,000 or more,
or affect the expenses of the


Our File No.
A-94-014
Page
6
business by $2,500 or more,
the business tenant will not be
considered a disqualifying financial interest of yours.
Thus,
you may participate in the decisions so long as they
will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on your real property or your sources of income as set forth
above.
The “Public Generally” Exception
An official does not have a financial interest and may
participate in governmental decisions if the effect on the
official’s economic interests is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally.
(Section 87103; Regulation
18703.)
In your facts,
you mention that there are less than 300
residential units within the city.
While the project will
increase the number of housing units within the city,
you have not
provided facts to indicate that the implementation decisions
concerning the housing project will have a similar effect on your
economic interests as a significant segment of the public.
For
example,
in order for Regulation 18703 to apply to the facts you
have presented,
a significant segment of property owners,
presumably those approximately the same distance from the site,
would have to be affected in a similar manner as your property.
If you have any further questions regarding this, matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322_5901.6
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel,
Legal Division
6
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively,
copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of
10c per page.

selection of the developer for the project?

on your real property or your sources of
income.

QUESTION: May you participate in decisions of the Irwindale Community
Redevelopment Agency Board of Directors to develop guidelines for
construction of
a
Low— and Moderate—Income housing project and for
selection of the developer for the project?

CONCLUSION: You may participate in the decisions so long as the decisions
will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on your real property or your sources of
income.
